description.task: Use SAVEPOINT and ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT within a transaction to undo only part of the work.
description.context: A SAVEPOINT marks a point in a transaction to which you can rollback without losing all the work done so far. It enables fine-grained error recovery within a long transaction.
instructions: Run the full BEGIN...COMMIT block with savepoints.
The inner SELECT (inside transaction) shows items 1-5 (webcam rolled back).
Final SELECT after COMMIT shows same 5 items.
Add a comment: can you ROLLBACK TO a savepoint that was defined BEFORE the current savepoint?
description.hint: Yes — you can ROLLBACK TO any savepoint defined earlier in the same transaction. The later savepoints are discarded when you rollback past them.
description.expectedOutput:
After ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT after_monitor: items 1-5 (webcam removed)
After COMMIT: 5 items persist

description.task: Use savepoints to process a batch of records — skipping failed records while preserving successful ones.
description.context: In batch processing, you may want to commit successfully processed records even if some fail. Savepoints per record allow you to skip bad records without rolling back the entire batch.
instructions: Run the entire batch transaction (Record 3 is commented out — simulating skip).
Uncomment Record 3 insert and its ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT to see the skip pattern fully.
Add a comment: in a real batch import of 10,000 rows, how would you implement this per-row savepoint pattern in application code?
description.hint: In application code, wrap each record insert in a try-catch. On exception: ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT before_record_N, log the error, then continue to the next record. Finally COMMIT at the end.
description.expectedOutput:
3 products: Laptop(1), Mouse(2), Keyboard(4)
Record 3 skipped — no 'Bad Item' in table

description.task: Demonstrate RELEASE SAVEPOINT which removes a savepoint (freeing memory) while keeping all work done since the savepoint.
description.context: RELEASE SAVEPOINT destroys the savepoint marker but does NOT rollback any work. It is used to clean up savepoints that are no longer needed — helpful in long transactions to avoid memory bloat.
instructions: Run the full transaction including RELEASE SAVEPOINT.
Run the final SELECT — all 3 tasks should be 'done'.
In a comment: why must you NOT confuse RELEASE SAVEPOINT with ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT?
Add a comment: what happens if you try to ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT task_a_done after releasing it?
description.hint: RELEASE SAVEPOINT is just bookkeeping — it removes the savepoint reference but keeps all DML changes. Trying to ROLLBACK TO a released savepoint will throw an error: 'savepoint does not exist'.
description.expectedOutput:
All 3 tasks: status = 'done'
RELEASE does not undo Task A's update
